story:
Ava has this weekly ritual of meeting strangers from dating apps. I had a bad feeling about this week's guy from the start. Call it intuition. I told her not to trust apps. She laughed, giddy as a kid, and showed me his photos. Horatio, she said, like it was the most romantic thing she'd ever heard. I questioned the name, the pictures, all of it. She bounced on my bed like it was a fairy tale. Now I wish I'd tried harder to stop her.

Night. No stars, no moon. I follow a pin drop Ava sent me—I check it three times. It leads to the morgue. It doesn't make sense, but I go anyway. Terracotta path, heavy steel doors, air that turns to ice the second I step inside. Bright lights. White tiles. The hush is so complete I can hear my shoes squeak. A wall of metal doors stretches in front of me like lockers in a school. Too many for a town this small, I think, and the thought makes me colder.

I whisper Ava's name. Nothing. My stomach knots. I start opening doors, one after another. Empty trays slide out with a hollow whoosh, like the building is sighing at me. Panic rises. Then I hear it—a faint, tinny sound from across the room. I run.

I pull a door open and freeze. It's Ava. She looks almost dead. Purple eyelids. Skin drained of color, veins a dull gray. Cuts. Bruises. Chains coil around her wrists, padlocks pinning her to the tray's metal rail. I touch her cheek. Frozen. The kind of cold that hides in the bone. He hurt you, I whisper, not sure if I'm talking to her or to myself. I grab her shoulders. Her head turns the smallest amount. Her eyes stay closed.

Willow? It's a breath of a voice.

I'm here. I tell her I'll get her out. I try the locks with my fingers, then sprint to the workbench for a scalpel. I jam it into the lock, wiggle, pray—nothing budges. Her face is empty, like a candle that burned out long ago.

Willow, she says again, and I lean close.

Run.

The word drops like ice. Startled, I jerk my hand back and the scalpel slashes across my thigh as my grip slips. It stings, but fear is louder. I stumble back—and hands catch me from behind. I don't hear a single step.

A mist sprays into my eyes. Fire explodes behind my eyelids. I scream. I thrash, blind, wild, but an arm hooks around my throat and squeezes. I claw at the grip, my nails raking skin, but it doesn't loosen. A tray slides open somewhere. I'm lifted and thrown onto metal. My spine rings with pain. Through the burning tears and pain, I catch a blurred glimpse of something dark covering the attacker's face before my vision fails completely. A hand slams my wrist to the tray. Chains bite. A padlock settles with a heavy certainty. I fight with my free hand. A sharp sting pricks my arm. Numbness floods in, pins and needles turning to heavy stone.

The tray begins to slide. Cold air creeps over me. I can't move. I can't see. Fear eats all the space in my chest. The door hinges creak. Just before the final click, a deep, husky voice breathes into the dark.

Sleep tight, Willow.
